coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots hydrogels for highly efficient solar steam generation
coredata.dc:creator: Allahbakhsh, Ahmad
coredata.dc:description: Solar evaporation is a sustainable and green strategy to relieve the growing global freshwater crisis. In this strategy, a solar irradiation absorber platform absorbs the sunlight and converts it into heat for water evaporation. Herein, a new class of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) hydrogels has been developed for highly efficient solar water evaporation via the heat localization mechanism. N-GQDs hydrogels are fabricated via the hydrothermal self-assembly process. Using N-GQDs with different lateral size ranges as the precursor, hydrogels with different structural properties and solar water evaporation efficiencies are prepared. The highest achieved solar steam generation efficiency in this work is 89.7% obtained by employing N-GQDs with the smallest lateral size range in the precursor solution. The steam generation efficiency of hydrogels reduces with increasing the lateral size of synthesized N-GQDs in precursors. The colloidal morphology, high hydrophilicity, hierarchical pore structure, and low thermal conductivity of prepared N-GQDs hydrogels result in the high performance of these structures in solar water evaporation applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Solar steam generation
Hydrogel
Heat localization
Porous nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Impacts of the synthesis duration on the structure of N-GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author would like to thank Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) for its supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a and b) and HRTEM (c and d) images of 3h-N-GQDs (a and c) and 12h-N-GQDs (b and d).
DLS size distribution graphs of 3h-N-GQDs (a), 6h-N-GQDs (b), 9h-N-GQDs (c), and 12h-N-GQDs (d).
AFM topographic (a and c) and phase change (b and d) images of 3h-N-GQDs (a and b) and 12h-N-GQDs (c and d).
FTIR spectra (a and b) and XRD patterns (c and d) of 3h-N-GQDs (a and c) and 12h-N-GQDs (b and d).
UV–vis spectra of prepared N-GQDs samples (a), and fitted UV–vis spectra of 3h-N-GQDs (b), 6h-N-GQDs (c), 9h-N-GQDs (d), and 12h-N-GQDs (e).

Water evaporation rates and efficiencies of samples under one sun illumination (a), mass change of water (water mass change under dark environment subtracted) with and without hydrogels under one sun illumination (b), the surface temperature of evaporation systems with and without hydrogels under one sun illumination (c), and IR thermal images of evaporation systems with hydrogels after 1 h of illumination (d).
A schematic of colloidal primary and secondary nanoparticles and the hierarchical porosity in the structure of N-GQDs hydrogels.
A comparison between the absorption behaviors of all samples (a), as well as absorption spectra of 3h-A (b), 6h-A (c), 9h-A (d), and 12h-A (e).

Recycling and water evaporation performance of the 3h-A sample through repeated solar illumination (1 kW/m2) for 0.5 h cycles (the highlighted background region represents the evaporation efficiency range of 85–100%).
Textural properties of porous N-GQDs samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: in this table, HT: heat treated at 1000 °C, ρ: density, SLangmuir: Langmuir surface area, SBET: BET surface area, Vt: total pore volume, Sμ: micropore surface area, Vμ: micropore volume.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Solar water evaporation performance of different graphene-based porous materials under one sun illumination.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: in this table, rGO: reduced graphene oxide, CNT: carbon nanotubes, SA: sodium alginate, EDA: ethylenediamine, ρ: density, η: solar water evaporation efficiency.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots hydrogels for highly efficient solar steam generation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials and Polymer Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Hakim Sabzevari University, Sabzevar, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials and Polymer Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials and Polymer Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Hakim Sabzevari University, Sabzevar, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar evaporation is a sustainable and green strategy to relieve the growing global freshwater crisis. In this strategy, a solar irradiation absorber platform absorbs the sunlight and converts it into heat for water evaporation. Herein, a new class of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) hydrogels has been developed for highly efficient solar water evaporation via the heat localization mechanism. N-GQDs hydrogels are fabricated via the hydrothermal self-assembly process. Using N-GQDs with different lateral size ranges as the precursor, hydrogels with different structural properties and solar water evaporation efficiencies are prepared. The highest achieved solar steam generation efficiency in this work is 89.7% obtained by employing N-GQDs with the smallest lateral size range in the precursor solution. The steam generation efficiency of hydrogels reduces with increasing the lateral size of synthesized N-GQDs in precursors. The colloidal morphology, high hydrophilicity, hierarchical pore structure, and low thermal conductivity of prepared N-GQDs hydrogels result in the high performance of these structures in solar water evaporation applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar evaporation is an effective strategy to use renewable and sustainable solar energy to address the global clean freshwater shortage crisis [1,2]. In this strategy, a solar absorber platform is used to harvest solar energy, and the harvested thermal energy can then be used to generate steam via technologies such as heat localization [2]. In the heat localization mechanism, the harvested thermal energy is localized in a spot to minimize the loss of energy [3]. The accumulation of harvested heat increases the local temperature of water molecules in a thin layer of water and leads to steam generation [3]. Consequently, porous nanostructures with high solar absorption, high porosity, and low thermal conductivity can be ideal platforms for solar water evaporation applications; as these nanostructures can present not only high solar energy harvesting capabilities and water entrapment in their porous structure but also energy localization and high water evaporation efficiencies [4–6].

The evaporation temperature and the energy removal rate in a porous water evaporation system strongly depend on the microstructure of the porous medium, as the volume of the water contained in the pores of the porous medium controls the evaporation process [7]. Different porous structures such as polymer-absorber/wood composites [8], metal-foam-based structures [9], carbon fiber-based porous systems [10,11], and nanostructures based on carbonous nanomaterials [12,13] can be used to develop solar steam generation systems. Among these structures, carbonous porous materials have recently attracted great attention for solar evaporation applications, due to their high solar absorbance, open-cell porosity, tunable structure, and designable surface chemistry [12].

Graphene-based porous nanostructures are ultralightweight materials with a carbonous skeleton and interconnected pores and high light absorbance capabilities [1]. Moreover, graphene-based porous nanostructures can present a wide range of hydrophilicity and thermal conductivity, depending on the precursor type, chemical structure, and fabrication conditions [14,15]. Such a tunable structure has made graphene-based porous nanostructures perfect platforms for solar water evaporation applications [16,17]. In addition, solar-to-thermal conversion efficiency and thermal conductivity of graphene-based porous nanostructures can be designed by controlling the structure of these three-dimensional materials [18] and by doping the graphene-based skeleton of these materials with heteroatoms like nitrogen atoms [19].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are disk-like graphene-based nanomaterials with a lateral size range that is low enough to present the quantum confinement and edge effects [20]. It has been shown that the contribution of the quantum confinement in GQDs increases significantly when the lateral size of GQDs is less than 100 nm [20]. The optical properties of GQDs are one of the main advantages of these nanomaterials, which are known to be directly related to their size and structure [21,22]. GQDs and nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) are known to have interesting photo-to-thermal energy conversion capabilities, particularly in ultraviolet, near-infrared, and infrared electromagnetic wave regions [23,24]. Up to now, this important characteristic of GQDs has been extensively employed in photothermal therapy applications [24], and GQDs have hardly been considered as candidates to improve the photo-to-thermal energy conversion performance of solar evaporation systems [25,26].

Here, N-GQDs hydrogels are introduced as a new class of porous solar steam generation platforms with high solar water evaporation efficiencies. Different N-GQDs hydrogels are prepared from N-GQDs precursor solutions with diverse N-GQDs lateral size ranges through the hydrothermal aggregation process. The low thermal conductivity of these hydrogels leads to the heat localization and the temperature increase of water molecules pumped inside the hierarchical interconnected pore network of prepared N-GQDs hydrogels. Results presented here suggest that N-GQDs hydrogels are effective solar steam generation platforms and great candidates to develop solar-based water purification devices.
l-ascorbic acid (crystalline powder, LAA) was bought from Acros Organics, and branched polyethylenimine (Mw ~800, PEI) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

A new modified hydrothermal synthesis method was used in this study for the down-to-top synthesis of N-GQDs from PEI precursor aqueous solutions. The lateral dimension range of N-GQDs was controlled by changing the time of the hydrothermal process. In a typical synthesis process of PEI-derived N-GQDs, a 50 mg/ml solution of PEI in deionized water was first prepared by dissolving PEI in water via a magnet stirrer at room temperature. The PEI solution was then stirred for 10 more minutes. Then, about 15 ml of the PEI solution was poured into a stainless steel autoclave (Teflon-lined). The autoclave was sealed and heated for a specific period at 180 °C using an electronic oven. Hydrothermal processes were carried out for 3, 6, 9, and 12 h, and the synthesized N-GQDs using these process durations were labeled as 3h-N-GQDs, 6h-N-GQDs, 9h-N-GQDs, and 12h-N-GQDs, respectively. The synthesized N-GQDs solutions were then centrifuged using a Sigma 3-30KS centrifuge and the supernatant parts of each solution were used for the fabrication of hydrogels.

Hydrogels were fabricated through the self-assembly reduction of N-GQDs according to the method reported in the literature [27]. In a typical self-assembly process of N-GQDs hydrogels, LAA was first dissolved in synthesized N-GQDs solutions (3h-N-GQDs, 6h-N-GQDs, 9h-N-GQDs, or 12h-N-GQDs) with an LAA:N-GQDs ratio of 3:1. Then, 15 ml of the prepared LAA/N-GQDs was sealed in the stainless steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was placed in an electronic oven and was heated for 12 h at 190 °C. Afterward, the autoclave was naturally cooled down and the fabricated N-GQDs hydrogel was removed from the autoclave. The fabricated hydrogel was then placed in distilled water and solvent changed for a one week (see Fig. S1 for images of samples). To reach solid samples required for characterization purposes, hydrogels were freeze-dried at −55 °C for 24 h via an ALPHA 1–2 LDplus laboratory freeze dryer.

The setup depicted in Fig. S2 was employed in this study to investigate the evaporation rate of samples. The setup (Fig. S2) consisted of a sun simulator, a laboratory balance (A&D HR-200), an Infrared thermometer (UNI-T UT303A), and a thermal imaging camera (SATIR Hotfind). The sun simulator device used in this study was a multi-light-source device with halogen (100 W) and xenon (60 W) light sources (equipped with optical filters, AM 1.5G). A BloorSpec power meter was used to check the power density of the sun simulator and the accuracy of the power provided by the device was further confirmed using a standard silicon solar cell.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained via a 200 kV FEI Tecnai F20 microscope. Moreover, a 150 kV Philips CM30 microscope was used to obtain transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results. In addition, a Dualscope DS 95–200 DME atomic force microscope was used to carry out atomic force microscopy (AFM) tests. Samples were prepared on freshly cleaved mica and the non-contact mode with a scan rate of 5 μm/s was used. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) tests were carried out on a Perkin-Elmer spectrometer (ASTM E 1252–98). In addition, a Philips PW1730 diffractometer (source: Cu, λ = 1.54056 Å) was used to obtain X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns at 40 kV and 30 mA.

Raman spectra of dried samples were recorded on a Teksan TakRam N1–541 spectrometer at 532 nm. In addition, Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) tests were conducted on a NanoDrop 2000 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. A TESCAN Mira 3 microscope was used to obtain field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) results at 15 kV. Moreover, a BELSORP MINI II analyzer was used to record nitrogen (N2) adsorption/desorption isotherms at 77 K. Dried samples were degassed before analysis under vacuum at 120 °C for 12 h. Mercury (Hg) porosimetry measurements were carried out on a Thermo Finnigan Pascal 440 porosimeter. Washburn and buckling theories were applied to calculated data to obtain pore diameters. The hot-plate method described in the literature was used to report thermal conductivity values [28,29]. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) results were reported on a Nanotrac Wave II Analyzer at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impacts of the synthesis duration on the structure of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, N-GQDs with different lateral size ranges were fabricated by controlling the interval of the carbonization process. The duration of the carbonization process had a direct impact on the lateral size of N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 . The average size of N-GQDs synthesized during 3 h of the hydrothermal process (3h-N-GQDs) was about 6 nm (Fig. 1a). As the duration of the synthesis process increased to 12 h (12h-N-GQDs), the average lateral size of synthesized N-GQDs increased to around 100 nm (Fig. 1b), suggesting the conversion of N-GQDs to nitrogen-doped graphene oxide (N-doped GO) with very low lateral size-ranges of 100–300 nm. It should be noted here that only N-GQDs with specific shapes were used for the calculation of diameter histograms in Fig. 1, and N-GQDs with larger lateral sizes were also observed in TEM results of 12h-N-GQDs (see Fig. 1b). HRTEM results were used to further investigate the structure of synthesized N-GQDs. HRTEM images of both 3h-N-GQDs (Fig. 1c) and 12h-N-GQDs (Fig. 1d) confirmed the high crystalline nature of synthesized N-GQDs with a lattice fringe of 2.4 Å, which corresponds to the (1120) hcp plane of graphene and GQDs [30,31].

The lateral size range of synthesized N-GQDs was further studied via DLS results, as shown in Fig. 2 . The lateral size range of the 3h-N-GQDs sample was 1–10 nm (Fig. 2a), which was lower than the reported lateral size range of N-GQDs with the quantum confinement effects (under 100 nm). The lateral size of synthesized N-GQDs sample in DLS results increased with the time of the hydrothermal process and lateral size ranges of 50–200, 100–600, and 200–700 nm were observed for 6h-N-GQDs, 9h-N-GQDs, and 12h-N-GQDs, respectively. Consequently, N-GQDs with different lateral size ranges were successfully synthesized by controlling the duration of the hydrothermal synthesis process. Moreover, the ionization of edge carboxyl groups in the structure of synthesized N-GQDs resulted in a zeta potential of around −22 mV (at neutral pH) for 3h-N-GQDs, which was close to the reported zeta potential of N-GQDs with the same lateral size-range in the literature [32,33].

AFM topography images were used to investigate the thickness of synthesized N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 3 . The thickness of synthesized 3h-N-GQDs in AFM height distribution calculations was about 0.5–0.7 nm (inset in Fig. 1a). This thickness range for synthesized N-GQDs is in close accordance with the observed low thickness of N-GQDs in HRTEM results (Fig. 1c) and the reported thickness range of N-GQDs in the literature (~0.5–1.5 nm [34–36]). An increase in the thickness of synthesized N-GQDs was observed when the duration of the hydrothermal process was increased to 12 h (Fig. 3c). The thickness of synthesized 12h-N-GQDs was in the range of 1–2.5 nm, suggesting the formation of few-layered N-doped GO nanoparticles (the thickness of GO nanosheets ~1.5 nm [37]). Such a thickness range was in accordance with TEM results of 12h-N-GQDs (insets in Fig. 1b) that depicted the formation of few-layered nanosheet-like N-doped GO sheets with lateral size ranges much greater than N-GQDs, but still considerably less than conventional GO nanosheets.

FTIR and XRD results (Fig. 4 ) were used to investigate the impacts of the extent of the carbonization process on the surface chemistry and microstructure of synthesized N-GQDs, respectively. FTIR results confirmed the presence of functional groups such as epoxy, peroxide, and carboxyl (νC-O α-region at ~1030 cm−1 and νC-O γ-region at ~1470 cm−1 [38]), ketone (νC-O β-region at ~1117 cm−1 [38]), and hydroxyl (νOH at ~3400 cm−1 [39]) groups in the structure of both 3h-N-GQDs (Fig. 4a) and 12h-N-GQDs (Fig. 4b). Moreover, the strong band observed at ~1650 cm−1 can be related to the overlapping of the stretching vibrations of CN and CO bonding in the structure of synthesized N-GQDs [30].

XRD patterns of both 3h-N-GQDs (Fig. 4c) and 12h-N-GQDs (Fig. 4d) were similar to the XRD pattern of GQDs reported in the literature [27]. A wide 002 peak representing the interplanar spacing of about 0.4 nm was observed in the XRD patterns of 3h-N-GQDs and 12h-N-GQDs. Compared to the interplanar spacing of GQDs (~0.34 nm [27]), the observed increase in the interplanar spacing of N-GQDs can be related to the expansion of spacing between basal planes due to the replacement of carbon atoms by nitrogen atoms in the structure of N-GQDs [30]. Compared to 3h-N-GQDs, the full width at half-maximum (FWHM) value of the main fitted peak (Lorentzian function) in the XRD pattern of 12h-N-GQDs reduced. Such a decrease in the FWHM value of the 002 peak in the XRD pattern of N-GQDs can be related to a reduction in the number of defects in the graphitic structure of synthesized N-GQDs [30].

UV–vis spectroscopy results were used to investigate the role of the synthesis duration on the optical properties of synthesized N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 5 . UV–vis spectra of synthesized N-GQDs (Fig. 5a) were fitted using three functions representing one band related to the π → π* transition (labeled as †) followed by two overlapping bands of n → π* transitions (labeled as ⁎ and ‡) in the structure of N-GQDs (Fig. 5b). The first band at ~230 nm was assigned to the band to band π → π* transitions of sp2 carbon atoms in the skeleton of N-GQDs [27]. The position of this absorbance band remained almost constant as the duration of the hydrothermal process increased from 2 to 12 h (peak † in Fig. 5b-e). Two remaining overlapping bands at wavelength values of around 314 and 370 nm in the UV–vis spectrum of 3h-N-GQDs were related to n → π* transitions of CO (COOH edge groups [27]) and conjugated CN/C=N bonds [40].

Although no significant change in the position of the fitted band labeled as ⁎ was observed when the extent of the hydrothermal carbonization process increased from 2 h to 6, 9, and 12 h, a blue shift in the position of the fitted band labeled as ‡ was observed in Fig. 5b–e. Since the longer carbonization process increased the lateral size of N-GQDs, and larger N-GQDs lateral sizes are expected to have more edges, the fitted band labeled as ‡ can be related to CO bonding of carbonyl and carboxyl groups [41]. With increasing the time of the carbonization process to 12 h (Fig. 5e), an increase in the intensity of the ‡ band was observed (compared to the intensity of other fitted bands). Moreover, the position of this band shifted to the reported position of the n → π* transitions assigned to CO bonding in the structure of GO (expected at around 290 nm [42]).

Synthesized N-GQDs solutions with different lateral sizes of N-GQDs were self-assembled into hydrogels through the hydrothermal reduction via ascorbic acid as the reducing agent. For characterization purposes, fabricated hydrogels were freeze-dried. N-GQDs hydrogels prepared from 3h-N-GQDs, 6h-N-GQDs, 9h-N-GQDs, and 12h-N-GQDs precursor solutions were freeze-dried and labeled as 3h-A, 6h-A, 9h-A, and 12h-A, respectively. Morphology of synthesized N-GQDs porous structures was studied using FESEM results, as shown in Fig. 6 . The structure of 3h-A was highly porous with colloidal/semi-spherical large particles consisting of primary colloidal nanoparticles (Fig. 6a). The morphology of prepared N-GQDs porous structures was similar to the morphology of GQDs hydrogels and cryogels reported in the literature [27].

The most important structural advantage of GQDs- and N-GQDs-based self-assembled porous nanostructures, compared to all available graphene-based porous structures, is the colloidal morphology and nanoporosity of these structures. Porous nanostructures based on solely graphene and graphene-based materials (without any additional component in the system) present a house of card arrangement and architecture of nanosheets [14]. Colloidal morphologies in this class of nanostructures can only be achieved by introducing polymers in the structure of graphene-based porous nanomaterials (composite or hybrid graphene/polymer porous nanostructures [14]), which reduce the content of graphene surface in the structure. GQDs and N-GQDs porous nanostructures are the only available porous structure with maximum surface characteristics of graphene-based nanostructures and colloidal morphologies. However, it should be noted here that, similar to GQDs hydrogels and cryogels [27], N-GQDs porous nanostructures prepared in this work presented very poor mechanical characteristics and structural stability.

An increase in the lateral size of N-GQDs in the precursor resulted in the formation of more merged regions in the structure of prepared N-GQDs porous materials. Merged regions in the structure of 6h-A were distinguishable in the FESEM images of this sample in Fig. 6b. The formation of these merged regions can lead to a decrease in the porosity of the system (mostly meso and microporosity), as large and small nanoparticles play an important role in the porosity and texture of N-GQDs porous materials. Compared to 6h-A, the morphology of samples prepared using 9h-N-GQDs and 12h-N-GQDs precursors (Fig. 6c and d, respectively) depicted more merged regions and larger nanoparticles, suggesting the effective role of N-GQDs size range in the precursor solution in the final morphology of prepared hydrogels.

N2 adsorption/desorption results were used to investigate the morphology and porosity of prepared N-GQDs porous structures, as shown in Fig. 7 . The N2 adsorption isotherm of 3h-A (Fig. 7a) was a Type-V adsorption isotherm, representing a porous structure with micro and mesoporosity. The specific surface area of 3h-A in N2 adsorption studies was ~181 m2/g, which was lower than the surface area expected for this class of porous nanostructures, as the content of adsorbed N2 in the structure of 3h-A was more than 450 cm3/g in Fig. 7a. The same behavior was observed in N2 adsorption studies of both GQDs- and novolac-based aerogels [27,28]. The presence of high contents of surface functional groups on the available surface of these porous structures has been considered as a possible explanation for such behavior [27,28].

The 3h-A sample was heat-treated to 1000 °C under the nitrogen atmosphere (with a heating rate of 10 °C/min) to study the role of these functional groups on the N2 adsorption surface area of samples (Fig. S3). The specific surface area and the density of the heat-treated 3h-A sample were about 523 m2/g and 0.0093 g/cm3, respectively (Table 1 ). Moreover, Hg porosimetry results of the original and heat-treated 3h-A samples (Fig. S4) confirmed specific surface areas of around 549 and 609 m2/g, respectively. Therefore, the specific surface area of all prepared hydrogel samples is expected to be in the range of 500–600 m2/g, considering Hg porosimetry and N2 adsorption results of both heat-treated and original 3h-A samples, as well as the density range of all prepared porous N-GQDs samples (Table 1).

The N2 adsorption isotherms of 6h-A (Fig. 7b), 9h-A (Fig. 7c), and 12h-A (Fig. 7d) samples were all Type-IV isotherms with adsorption/desorption hysteresis at relative pressures between 0.8 and 1.0, representing porous structures with the dominating mesoporosity [43]. This was in accordance with the observed increase in the volume of mesopores in the BJH diagrams of these samples (insets in Fig. 7). Moreover, the calculated specific surface areas of 6h-A, 9h-A, and 12h-A samples were about 212, 211, and 120 m2/g, respectively (Table S1). Considering FESEM studies, the decreasing trend of the specific surface area of porous samples with increasing the lateral size of N-GQDs in the precursor solution can be related to the variation of the size of colloidal particles and the observed increase in the content of merged regions in the structure of 6h-A, 9h-A and 12h-A samples.

XRD results were used to investigate the impacts of the gelation process on the structure of prepared N-GQDs porous samples, as shown in Fig. 8 . No significant change in the position or the relative intensity of the broad 002 peak in the XRD pattern of all porous samples was observed (compared to the intensity of the very broad 100 peak at 2θ ~ 43°). This indicates that all hydrogels were prepared through the same aggregation mechanism, similar to the aggregation mechanism described in the literature for the self-assembly of GQDs [27]. A combination of J-type and H-type π-π stacking aggregation mechanisms have been proposed in the literature as the aggregation mechanisms involved in the self-assembly of GQDs into colloidal aggregates through the hydrothermal reduction process [27,44].

FTIR results were used to study the surface chemistry of prepared N-GQDs nanoporous structures, as shown in Fig. 9 . The band expected at wavenumbers around 1700 cm−1, which represent the stretching vibrations of C=O/C=N bonding of N atoms and oxygen groups such as peroxide and carboxyl, almost disappeared in the FTIR spectrum of 3h-A (Fig. 9a), and only a shoulder to the band related to the stretching vibrations of CC bonding (the skeletal vibrations of N-GQDs) was observed. Moreover, compared to the intensity of νC=C band, the relative intensity of bands assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO bonding in α and β regions (mostly epoxide groups) and hydroxyl groups reduced noticeably in Fig. 9a. These observations can be explained by the impacts of the hydrothermal reduction process and π-π stacking on the structure of N-GQDs through the formation of colloidal nanoparticles.

The hydrothermal reduction process of GQDs is reported in the literature to mostly result in the removal of hydroxyl and epoxy groups from the basal plane and edges of GQDs [45]. A comparison between FTIR spectra of 3h-A (Fig. 9a) and 3h-N-GQDs (Fig. 4a) suggests that a similar phenomenon took place during the hydrothermal reduction of N-GQDs into hydrogels. FTIR spectra of 6h-A (Fig. 9b), 9h-A (Fig. 9c), and 12h-A (Fig. 9d) samples were almost identical to the FTIR spectrum of 3h-A (Fig. 9a), suggesting that the hydrothermal reduction of all precursor N-GQDs solutions resulted in the same stacking mechanism and formation of porous structures with same surface chemistries.

The only observed main difference between FTIR spectra of porous samples in Fig. 9 was in the intensity of the shoulder band at ~1690 cm−1 assigned to C=O/C=N bonding (labeled as ‖), which increased with the lateral size of N-GQDs. This can be described by the number of edge carboxyl groups in the structure of N-GQDs, which increased by the lateral size of N-GQDs at longer N-GQDs synthesis durations. FTIR spectra of all studied samples suggest the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of prepared N-GQDs nanoporous structures, suggesting the formation of N-GQDs hydrogels with hydrophilic surfaces. Moreover, the hydrophilic nature of prepared N-GQDs porous structures was further confirmed through water droplet contact angle studies (Fig. S5). An increase in the lateral size of N-GQDs in the precursor solutions resulted in not only an increase in the instantaneous contact angle but also the longer spread of the water droplet on the surface of nanoporous samples. The hydrophilicity of prepared N-GQDs hydrogels plays an important role in the evaporation efficiency of these structures, as high hydrophilicity of samples results in the high water flow from bulk water to the evaporation structure [46].

Raman results of prepared N-GQDs nanoporous materials were used to further investigate the impacts of the reduction process on the structural properties of samples, as shown in Fig. 10 . Raman spectra of N-GQDs porous structures were fitted via a six-peak model with three Lorentzian (D, G, and D′ bands) and three Gaussian functions (D1, D2, and D3 bands) [30,47]. The fitting parameters of these six Raman peaks and the intensity ratio of these bands are summarized in Table S2. The intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) has been considered as a measure for the nature of armchair and zigzag edge sites in the structure of GQDs [30,47]. High ID/IG ratios (>1.4–1.6) can represent the dominance of armchair edges in the structure of GQDs [30,47]. In addition, lower ID/IG ratios suggest lower edge defects, more crystallinity, and the presence of both armchair and zigzag edges in the structure of GQDs and N-GQDs [30,47].

The ID/IG ratio of all prepared N-GQDs porous samples was in the range of 0.6–0.8, with slight variations (Table S2). This range of ID/IG ratio is known to represent the formation of both armchair and zigzag edge sites and high crystallinity in the structure of N-GQDs hydrogels [30,47]. This is in close accordance with HRTEM results of N-GQDs in the precursor solutions, where the presence of highly ordered carbon atoms and high crystallinity in the structure of N-GQDs prepared through both short (3h-N-GQDs) and long (12h-N-GQDs) durations of the hydrothermal synthesis process was observed (Fig. 1c and d).

The intensity ratio of D1 and G bands (ID1/IG ratio) is another characteristic of the structure of N-GQDs that represent sp2-sp3 bonding at the edges and N-doping content in the structure of N-GQDs [30]. The ID1/IG ratio of 3h-A was greater than all other hydrogels, and an increase in the size of N-GQDs in the precursor solution resulted in a lower ID1/IG ratio, indicating the role of increased edge defects in the structure of hydrogels prepared from N-GQDs synthesized through longer hydrothermal synthesis duration.

No significant change was observed in the intensity ratio of D2 and G bands (ID2/IG) with the lateral size of N-GQDs in the precursor solution (Table S2). Since the ID2/IG ratio represent the content of COOH/C–OH functional groups in the structure of N-GQDs, this behavior can be considered as additional evidence for FTIR observations, where no significant change in the content of carboxyl groups in different hydrogels was observed. In addition, the intensity ratio of D3 and G bands (ID3/IG) in the Raman spectrum of GQDs is known to be a measure for the N-doping induced higher electronic charge in the graphitic network and as a result, an indicator of N-doping content in N-GQDs [30]. The observed slight increase in the ID3/IG ratio of hydrogels with larger lateral sizes can be related to the higher N-doping of N-GQDs.

Consequently, not only the surface of colloidal nanoparticles in the structure of N-GQDs hydrogels was covered with oxygen-containing functional groups, but also high contents of doped N atoms were indicated in the structure of hydrogels. These two structural factors, as well as the nanoporous structure of N-GQDs hydrogels with hierarchical pores ranging from micro to macroporosities, provide an effective platform for solar-to-thermal energy harvesting and conversion, as well as solar water evaporation through the heat localization mechanism.

Solar steam generation performance of prepared N-GQDs hydrogels under 1.0 sun irradiation was studied via evaporation rate and efficiency calculations, as shown in Fig. 11 . Solar steam generation efficiency values (η) were calculated from the water evaporation rate of samples (m) under one sun irradiation via the following equation [48,49]:
η =
m
h LV
C opt
q i
where Copt is the optical concentration and qi is the solar irradiation (1 kW/m2) [48,49]. Moreover, hLV is the total enthalpy of the liquid-to-vapor phase change that can be calculated from the following equation [48,49]:
h LV
= λ + C Δ T
where λ is the latent heat of phase change (temperature dependent), C is the specific heat capacity of water (4.2 kJ/kg·K), and ΔT is the temperature increase of water. The 3h-A hydrogel presented a solar steam generation efficiency of 89.7%, which was the highest solar steam generation efficiency among prepared N-GQDs hydrogels (Fig. 11a). This solar evaporation efficiency is among the highest reported water evaporation efficiency for systems with graphene-based monolith porous nanostructures as the solar absorption component [19,48,50]. Moreover, the highest water mass change of systems consisting of water and N-GQDs hydrogels was obtained when 3h-A was used as the solar-to-thermal energy harvesting platform (Fig. 11b). The solar steam generation efficiency of N-GQDs hydrogels decreased with increasing the lateral size of N-GQDs in the precursor solution. Moreover, the highest temperature increase rate was obtained by employing 3h-A hydrogel as the solar absorption component in the system (Fig. 11c).

As the lateral size of N-GQDs increased in the precursor solution, the temperature increase rate of the solar water evaporation systems decreased. Moreover, the temperature profile of studied solar steam generation systems under one sun illumination (Fig. 11d) revealed that the temperature range in all systems was about 40–53 °C after 1 h of illumination (see Fig. S6 for thermal images of systems before and after illumination under one sun).

The observed highly efficient solar steam generation performance of prepared N-GQDs hydrogels can be related to several structural features of these hydrogels. The most important advantage of N-GQDs hydrogels is the colloidal porous structure of these hydrogels that consists of nanoparticles with different size ranges, as shown schematically in Fig. 12 . This specific arrangement leads to the formation of a hierarchical interconnected network of pores in the structure of N-GQDs hydrogels. In such structures, micropores mostly present in the interspacing of primary nanoparticles, and mesopores/macropores form between secondary nanoparticles. To the best of the author's knowledge, this is the only porous platform with colloidal nanoparticles consisting of graphene-based structures.

In these structures, a combination of J-type to the H-type aggregation of N-GQDs through the hydrothermal reduction process results in the formation of primary nanoparticles [27]. These nanoparticles then aggregate with the progress of the reduction process to form secondary nanoparticles. Since the size of primary nanoparticles was lower than 10 nm (primary nanoparticles in Fig. 12), micropores (pores with diameters lower than 2 nm) most probably formed between primary nanoparticles. Moreover, as the size of secondary nanoparticles was around 50–100 nm (secondary nanoparticles in Fig. 12), mesopores and macropores (pores with diameter between 2 and 50 nm and greater than 50 nm, respectively) most probably formed between secondary nanoparticles.

The high content of oxygen functional groups and doped N atoms can also be considered as an effective factor in the solar water evaporation performance of N-GQDs hydrogels. The main mechanism in the solar steam generation of N-GQDs hydrogels is the heat localization mechanism. Low thermal conductivity of the irradiation absorber platform in this mechanism results in the local temperature increase of water pumped in the pores of the absorber. The thermal conductivity of 3h-A is 0.012 ± 0.001 W/m·K, which is much lower than the thermal conductivity of graphene-based monolith porous nanostructures [14]. Phonon scattering due to the presence of structural impurities like oxygen-containing functional groups, high content of boundary scattering due to the low lateral size of N-GQDs in the structure of 3h-A, and the formation of localized phonon scattering sites due to doped N atoms can be considered as the main reasons for the low thermal conductivity of N-GQDs hydrogels. Such a low thermal conductivity of N-GQDs porous nanostructures leads to the efficient heat localization in these hydrogels.

Although the high solar irradiation absorption, efficient heat localization, and hydrophilicity, as well as the formation of an interconnected hierarchical network of pores, can be considered as the main advantages of N-GQDs hydrogels in solar evaporation applications, the mechanical properties of these hydrogels were extremely low. Such low structural stability is an important obstacle toward the successful application of these porous structures. Therefore, improving the mechanical properties and viscoelasticity of N-GQDs hydrogels will be an important objective for my future works in this field.

The presence of N-GQDs as the building units in the structure of N-GQDs porous nanostructures resulted in a high sun illumination absorption of these materials through a wide range of wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 13 . All prepared N-GQDs porous materials exhibited almost complete electromagnetic wave absorption in both near IR and UV–vis ranges. The irradiation of the sun below 1200 nm and in the UV–vis and near IR range is known to be with the highest energy [49]. Consequently, the high electromagnetic wave absorption performance of N-GQDs hydrogels in this range (Fig. 13) can also be considered as an effective factor in the highly efficient solar water evaporation performance of N-GQDs porous nanostructures prepared in this work. Moreover, a comparison between the illumination absorption of samples prepared in this work demonstrates that the lateral size of N-GQDs in the precursor solution had a negligible impact on the solar absorption performance of N-GQDs porous nanostructures. This behavior can be related to the similar structure and morphology of prepared N-GQDs hydrogels. As a result, the observed difference between the solar evaporation efficiency of prepared N-GQDs hydrogels (Fig. 11) can be related to the discussed differences between the phonon scattering level in the structure of samples and the heat localization efficiency of prepared N-GQDs porous nanostructures.

Graphene quantum dots are well-known quantum dots materials with strong electromagnetic wave absorption in the ranges of UV and visible. The high absorption performance of these materials is structurally due to π − π* transition in the sp2 basal plane of GQDs, similar to other graphene-based nanostructures [51,52]. Compared to GQDs and N-GQDs, aggregated N-GQDs in the structure of N-GQDs hydrogels present a wider range of illumination adsorption due to the impacts of the aggregation on the electromagnetic wave absorption performance of N-GQDs. Moreover, such aggregation is known to affect the deexcitation mechanism through the photoexcitation in the structure of N-GQDs [53]. Consequently, high photothermal conversion efficiencies were achieved by employing N-GQDs as the building units of N-GQDs porous nanostructures [53].

The stability of prepared N-GQDs porous nanostructures for the solar evaporation application was evaluated through 10 repeated illumination cycles of the 3h-A sample under one sun irradiation, as shown in Fig. 14 . Each 0.5 h irradiation step was followed by the drying of the sample for the next cycle [54,55]. Moreover, to compare the performance of the 3h-A sample through repeated illumination cycles, the efficiency range of 85–100% for the sample was calculated and presented as the highlighted region in Fig. 14. A relatively stable recycling performance was observed for N-GQDs porous nanostructures through cycles. The observed slight variation in the solar evaporation performance of the sample can be related to the fluctuations in humidity and temperature through different cycles [17].

A comparison between the solar water evaporation efficiencies of different graphene-based porous nanostructures prepared from solely graphene-based nanomaterials (nanocomposite and hybrid porous nanostructures are excluded) with almost similar efficiencies is presented in Table 2 . Compared to porous graphene and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) aerogels, N-GQDs porous nanostructures reported in this work (3h-A sample) presented much higher efficiencies at almost similar densities. More importantly, the hydrothermal pyrolysis process reported here for the fabrication of N-GQDs precursor solutions from PEI, as well as the hydrothermal preparation process used in this work for the fabrication of N-GQDs hydrogels were highly efficient and simple one-step processes.

Compared to the estimated fabrication cost of all graphene-based porous nanostructures listed in Table 2, the production cost of N-GQDs hydrogels was more than 99% lower (Table S2), due to the lower precursor costs. The main reported precursor for most graphene-based porous nanostructures applicable in solar steam generation applications is graphene oxide (GO), which should be mainly synthesized through the oxidation and mechanical exfoliation of graphite with the use of an oxidation system (mostly strong oxidizing agents such as potassium permanganate and sulfuric acid) [37,38]. Therefore, the final production cost of these porous nanostructures is mostly high, as most chemicals used through the oxidation process of graphite are costly [37,38].

N-GQDs solutions used in this study as the precursors for the fabrication of hydrogels can be synthesized through a simple one-step hydrothermal carbonization of PEI. Moreover, the precursor solution can be converted into three-dimensional nanoporous structures with solar evaporation efficiencies as high as 89.7% through a conventional hydrothermal reduction process by employing LAA as the reducing agent. Consequently, a low precursor cost of about $0.004 per cm3 of N-GQDs hydrogels was achieved in this work, which was more than 99% lower than the precursor cost of other graphene-based porous nanostructures with much lower solar evaporation efficiencies (see Table 2 and Table S2 for details). It should be noted here that only the precursor costs were considered in cost estimations in this work, as different production methods can be used for the fabrication of graphene-based porous nanostructures (see Supporting Information for cost estimation details).

In this work, N-GQDs hydrogels were prepared from a series of N-GQDs precursor solutions with different lateral size ranges of N-GQDs. Prepared N-GQDs hydrogels presented the colloidal morphology with a hierarchical interconnected network of pores with pore sizes in micro, meso, and macropore ranges. The structure of hydrogels consisted of spherical primary nanoparticles that formed large nanoparticles with micropores. Large nanoparticles merged as the lateral size range of N-GQDs in the precursor solution increased. Surface chemistry and Raman analysis revealed that oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs hydrogels remained after the hydrogel preparation process and the surface of hydrogels was highly hydrophilic. However, these functional groups were mostly carbonyl groups, as other functional groups partially reduced through the hydrothermal reduction process. A high water evaporation efficiency of 89.7% was achieved by employing 3h-A hydrogel as the solar absorbing component in this work. Increasing the lateral size of N-GQDs in the precursor solution reduced the solar steam generation efficiency of the system. The lowest evaporation efficiency was the evaporation efficiency of 12h-A hydrogel that was 65.2%. These observations were explained by considering the merge of large nanoparticles in the structure of hydrogels prepared from N-GQDs with larger lateral size ranges. The efficient solar steam generation behavior of the 3h-A hydrogel confirmed that N-GQDs hydrogels can be effective platforms to design solar water purification devices.

Ahmad Allahbakhsh: Conceptualization; Data curation; Formal analysis; Funding acquisition; Investigation; Methodology; Project administration; Resources; Software; Supervision; Validation; Visualization; Roles/Writing - original draft; Writing - review & editing.

Supplementary data to this article can be found online at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2021.115264.
